title: 👑 Elite Challenge ⭐⭐⭐⭐: Present Continuous vs Simple

questions.prompt: Read: "Sami works in a bank from Monday to Friday. But this week he is on holiday, so he is painting his bedroom." Which sentence is TRUE?

options.text: Sami is working in the bank today.
Sami paints bedrooms every day.
Sami is painting his bedroom this week.
Sami never works in a bank.

questions.explanation: "this week he is on holiday, so he is painting" — the continuous marks a temporary activity now, so (c) is true. (a) is false: he is on holiday this week, not at the bank. (b) misreads a one-off as a habit. (d) contradicts "Sami works in a bank from Monday to Friday".

options.text: She is making a cake right now.
I don't usually drink tea in the evening.
We are studying English this term.
He is needing some help with his bags.

questions.explanation: The error is (d): need is a stative verb and is not used in the continuous — He needs some help, not "is needing". (a) and (c) correctly use the continuous (right now, this term), and (b) correctly uses the simple for a habit (usually).

questions.prompt: Complete: "Normally the train ___ on time, but today it ___ very late."

options.text: is arriving / arrives
arrives / is arriving
arrive / is arriving
is arriving / is arriving

questions.explanation: "Normally" signals a habit → present simple (the train arrives), while "today" signals a temporary exception → present continuous (it is arriving). Only (b) matches each time word to its tense. The other pairs put the habit in the continuous or the now-action in the simple.

questions.prompt: Complete: "___ you ___ for the bus, or are you just standing here?"

options.text: Are / waiting
Do / waiting
Are / wait
Is / waiting

questions.explanation: A present-continuous question is be + subject + verb-ing: Are you waiting? "Do ... waiting" wrongly mixes do with -ing, "Are ... wait" drops the -ing, and "Is" is the wrong form of be for you. The hint "are you just standing here" confirms the continuous.

options.text: My parents are beliving the story.
The sun is rising in the east every day.
Why you are crying now?
The children are lying on the grass and looking at the clouds.

questions.explanation: Only (d) is fully correct: lie → lying (the -ie becomes -ying), with are for a plural now-action. (a) puts the stative verb believe in the continuous and misspells it (should be "believe the story"), (b) uses the continuous for the daily-truth "every day" (should be "rises"), and (c) misses the inversion (should be "Why are you crying?").

questions.prompt: Read: "It's 8 p.m. Lina is in the kitchen. There is a wonderful smell, and a pot is on the stove." What is Lina probably doing?

options.text: She is sleeping in her bed.
She is cooking dinner.
She cooks dinner every evening.
She is washing the car outside.

questions.explanation: The clues — in the kitchen, a wonderful smell, a pot on the stove — point to an action in progress now: she is cooking dinner (present continuous). (a) and (d) contradict "is in the kitchen". (c) is grammatically fine but uses the simple for a habit, which doesn't answer what she is doing at this moment.
